claim: LDL cholesterol has no involvement in the development of cardiovascular disease.

result.claim: LDL cholesterol has no involvement in the development of cardiovascular disease.
result.reasoning: The claim that LDL cholesterol has no involvement in the development of cardiovascular disease is overwhelmingly refuted by the evidence. Multiple sources explicitly state that LDL cholesterol is a causal agent in coronary heart disease and atherosclerosis. Paper ID 7662206, titled "A Century of Cholesterol and Coronaries: From Plaques to Genes to Statins," directly states that "Cholesterol-carrying low-density lipoprotein (LDL) is the essential causative agent of coronary heart disease" (prop_780, chunk_127). This paper further supports the causal link by noting that "Mutations that eliminate LDLRs raise LDL and cause heart attacks in childhood, whereas mutations that raise LDLRs reduce LDL and diminish heart attacks" (prop_782, prop_783, chunk_127). Another paper (Paper ID: 2095573) also explicitly states in its background that "LDL cholesterol has a causal role in the development of cardiovascular disease" (chunk_38). Furthermore, studies on mouse models (Paper ID: 14407673 and 970012) demonstrate that increased atherosclerosis is observed in mice with altered LDL receptor function or increased LDL levels (prop_2348, prop_2350, prop_1295, prop_19). The consistency and directness of these findings from multiple sources provide extremely strong evidence against the claim.

evidence.text: Low cardiorespiratory fitness is an established risk factor for cardiovascular disease (CVD) morbidity and mortality.
evidence.source: Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults.

evidence.text: The study used genome-wide association data from up to 11,685 participants with measures of circulating LDL-cholesterol concentrations across five studies.
evidence.source: LDL-cholesterol concentrations: a genome-wide association study

evidence.text: Meta-analysis showed an association of SNPs rs599839 and rs646776 with LDL-cholesterol concentrations.
evidence.source: LDL-cholesterol concentrations: a genome-wide association study

evidence.text: Cold exposure stimulated atherosclerotic plaque growth by increasing lipid deposition in LDL receptor(-/-) [Ldlr(-/-)] mice.
evidence.source: Cold Exposure Promotes Atherosclerotic Plaque Growth and Instability via UCP1-Dependent Lipolysis

evidence.text: Disorders of lysine acetylation may be correlated with obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.
evidence.source: Protein Lysine Acetylated/Deacetylated Enzymes and the Metabolism-Related Diseases

evidence.text: Increased atherosclerosis was observed in myeKlf2(-/-)Ldlr(-/-) mice compared with myeKlf2(+/+)Ldlr(-/-) littermates when the mice were fed a high fat and high cholesterol diet.
evidence.source: Myeloid-specific Krüppel-like factor 2 inactivation increases macrophage and neutrophil adhesion and promotes atherosclerosis.

evidence.text: Mutations that raise LDLRs reduce LDL and diminish heart attacks.
evidence.source: A Century of Cholesterol and Coronaries: From Plaques to Genes to Statins

evidence.text: A genome-wide association study of LDL-cholesterol concentrations was conducted.
evidence.source: LDL-cholesterol concentrations: a genome-wide association study

evidence.text: Cold-triggered food-intake-independent lipolysis increased plasma levels of small low-density lipoprotein (LDL) remnants in mice.
evidence.source: Cold Exposure Promotes Atherosclerotic Plaque Growth and Instability via UCP1-Dependent Lipolysis

evidence.text: Data from all seven populations were analyzed to determine the effect of each SNP on variations in circulating LDL-cholesterol concentrations.
evidence.source: LDL-cholesterol concentrations: a genome-wide association study

evidence.text: The increased atherosclerosis in myeKlf2(-/-)Ldlr(-/-) mice was associated with elevated presence of neutrophils and macrophages.
evidence.source: Myeloid-specific Krüppel-like factor 2 inactivation increases macrophage and neutrophil adhesion and promotes atherosclerosis.

evidence.text: LDLs are controlled by specific receptors (LDLRs) in the liver.
evidence.source: A Century of Cholesterol and Coronaries: From Plaques to Genes to Statins

evidence.text: SNPs rs599839 and rs646776 explained around 1% of the variation in circulating LDL-cholesterol concentrations.
evidence.source: LDL-cholesterol concentrations: a genome-wide association study

evidence.text: Mutations that eliminate LDLRs raise LDL and cause heart attacks in childhood.
evidence.source: A Century of Cholesterol and Coronaries: From Plaques to Genes to Statins

evidence.text: Increased plasma low density lipoprotein cholesterol concentration was significantly associated with improved patency at one year (85%) at values above the median compared with patency (68%) at values in the lower half of the range (p less than 0.02).
evidence.source: Influence of smoking and plasma factors on patency of femoropopliteal vein grafts.

evidence.text: Cholesterol-carrying low-density lipoprotein (LDL) is the essential causative agent of coronary heart disease.
evidence.source: A Century of Cholesterol and Coronaries: From Plaques to Genes to Statins

evidence.text: In 2015, high total cholesterol contributed 88·7 million (74·6 million to 105·7 million) global DALYs.
evidence.source: Global, regional, and national comparative risk assessment of 79 behavioural, environmental and occupational, and metabolic risks or clusters of risks, 1990–2015: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2015

evidence.text: Increased atherosclerosis was observed in myeKlf2(-/-)Ldlr(-/-) mice.
evidence.source: Myeloid-specific Krüppel-like factor 2 inactivation increases macrophage and neutrophil adhesion and promotes atherosclerosis.

evidence.text: SNPs rs599839 and rs646776 were associated with about 15% of an SD change in LDL cholesterol per allele, assuming an SD of 1 mmol/L.
evidence.source: LDL-cholesterol concentrations: a genome-wide association study

evidence.text: Two SNPs, rs599839 and rs4970834, showed genome-wide statistical association with LDL cholesterol at chromosomal locus 1p13.3.
evidence.source: LDL-cholesterol concentrations: a genome-wide association study
